question: What does the modern Android app architecture (Google's recommended) look like?
answer:
Google's recommended architecture (developer.android.com/architecture) is a layered, unidirectional-data-flow design:

• UI layer — Compose / View + ViewModel. Renders UI state, forwards user events. Owns no business logic.
• Domain layer (optional) — UseCase / Interactor classes that orchestrate complex flows or merge multiple repositories.
• Data layer — Repository per data type. Repositories aggregate sources (remote, cache, local DB) behind a clean API. Sources are split: RemoteDataSource (Retrofit), LocalDataSource (Room, DataStore).

UI observes state, never mutates it directly. State exposed as `StateFlow<UiState>`. Events flow through callbacks / SharedFlow. ViewModel has no Android framework dependencies — easy to unit-test.

question: What is the Repository pattern? Why use it on Android?
answer:
A Repository is a single class that owns access to a domain entity, hiding WHERE the data comes from (network, DB, cache, in-memory).

Why:
• Single source of truth — UI talks to Repository, not directly to network.
• Offline-first becomes natural — Repository may serve from DB while refreshing from network.
• Testable — substitute a fake Repository to test ViewModels.
• Cross-cutting concerns (caching strategy, retry, mapping) live in one place, not duplicated across screens.

Best practice: Repository returns DOMAIN models (clean Kotlin types), never DTOs. Mappers convert wire/db rows ↔ domain at the boundary.

question: What is unidirectional data flow (UDF) in Android UI?
answer:
UDF is a discipline where:
• State flows DOWN — from ViewModel → Composable / Fragment.
• Events flow UP — user actions → callback functions on ViewModel.
• UI never mutates state directly; it only requests changes through events.

Resulting structure:
• `data class UiState(...)` — single source of truth for the screen.
• `StateFlow<UiState>` exposed by the ViewModel.
• `fun onAction(action: UiAction)` (or individual methods) accept events.

Benefits: predictable, testable, easy to reason about, easy to log/trace, and aligned with Compose's declarative model.

question: What is Clean Architecture? How does it map to Android?
answer:
Clean Architecture (Robert C. Martin) splits code into concentric layers:
• Domain (innermost) — pure business rules. Pure Kotlin/Java. No Android, no networking, no DB.
• Use Cases / Interactors — orchestrate domain rules.
• Adapters / Data layer — map external data (DB, API) to domain models.
• Frameworks (outermost) — Android, Retrofit, Room, etc.

The Dependency Rule: dependencies point INWARD. Domain doesn't know about Android; data layer depends on domain interfaces.

On Android, this manifests as Gradle modules: `:domain`, `:data`, `:app` (or feature modules + `:core-domain`, `:core-data`). Pure-Kotlin domain modules speed up tests and avoid leaking framework concerns into business logic.

When it's worth it: medium+ apps, multiple teams, long-lived products. Overkill for prototypes.

question: How do you structure a multi-module Android project?
answer:
Common module taxonomy:

• `:app` — thin entry module that composes everything together (only NavGraph + DI wiring).
• `:feature:*` — feature modules (one per screen/flow) — Compose UI + ViewModel + screen-specific code.
• `:core:domain` — pure Kotlin business types + use cases.
• `:core:data` — Repository implementations, mappers, network/db sources.
• `:core:network` — Retrofit + interceptors + serialization config.
• `:core:database` — Room database + DAOs.
• `:core:designsystem` — colors, typography, common composables.
• `:core:common` — small utilities (date, formatting).

Benefits: parallel compilation (faster builds), enforced module boundaries via internal/public visibility, swap implementations in build variants, tests are per-module and fast.

Use `convention plugins` (in `buildSrc/`) to share Gradle config across modules.

question: How do you write testable Android code? Patterns and pitfalls.
answer:
Patterns that make Android code testable:

• Inject dependencies — never use singletons / static `Context.getInstance()` in business code. Pass collaborators via constructor.
• Wrap framework APIs behind interfaces — `Clock`, `Logger`, `LocaleProvider`, etc. so tests can substitute deterministic versions.
• Pure ViewModels — depend only on Kotlin types + repository interfaces. No Android dependencies = JVM-fast tests.
• Use coroutines test artifacts — `kotlinx-coroutines-test` provides `runTest`, `StandardTestDispatcher`, virtual time.
• Substitute fakes for repositories instead of mocks (mocks rot fast).
• For Compose UI tests, use `createComposeRule()` + `onNodeWithText(...)` / `onNodeWithTag(...)`.
• For instrumented tests, prefer `androidx.test.ext.junit` + Hilt test rules.

Pitfalls: testing implementation details, mocking what you don't own, testing through Activity (use ViewModel + UI separately).
